coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient photodegradation of ciprofloxacin by dual Z-scheme Bi2MoO6/GQDs/TiO2 heterojunction photocatalysts: mechanism analysis and pathway exploration
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Yuqing
Ding, Chunsheng
Guo, Jun
Gan, Wei
Chen, Peng
Zhang, Miao
Sun, Zhaoqi
coredata.dc:description: There is a high demand for photocatalysts that can efficiently degrade antibiotics; however, there are challenges in technological development related to charge transfer and light capture ability. In this study, ternary Bi2MoO6/GQDs/TiO2 (denoted as BGT; GQDs refer to graphene quantum dots) heterojunction photocatalysts with high ciprofloxacin (CIP) photodegradation efficiency were successfully prepared. BGT with a 0.20 mL GQD dispersion solution (BGT-2) exhibited the highest CIP photodegradation efficiency (90.21 %). The rate constant of BGT-2 was 15.49 × 10−3 min−1, which was 4.01, 4.16, and 9.22 times higher than those of Bi2MoO6/TiO2 (3.86 × 10−3 min−1), Bi2MoO6 (3.72 × 10−3 min−1), and TiO2 (1.68 ×10−3 min−1), respectively. The degradation efficiencies of amoxicillin and tetracycline hydrochloride were 78.98 % and 92.49 %, respectively. The effects of pH values, water sources, and inorganic anions on CIP degradation were systematically explored. In addition, three environmentally friendly degradation pathways were proposed based on liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) results and the toxicity evaluation of the various intermediates. Testing the optical and electrochemical properties of the catalysts showed that the synthesized dual Z-scheme heterojunctions can significantly improve the light capture ability and reduce the photogenerated electron-hole recombination rate, effectively addressing the challenges encountered with the use of photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient photodegradation of ciprofloxacin by dual Z-scheme Bi2MoO6/GQDs/TiO2 heterojunction photocatalysts: mechanism analysis and pathway exploration

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is a high demand for photocatalysts that can efficiently degrade antibiotics; however, there are challenges in technological development related to charge transfer and light capture ability. In this study, ternary Bi2MoO6/GQDs/TiO2 (denoted as BGT; GQDs refer to graphene quantum dots) heterojunction photocatalysts with high ciprofloxacin (CIP) photodegradation efficiency were successfully prepared. BGT with a 0.20 mL GQD dispersion solution (BGT-2) exhibited the highest CIP photodegradation efficiency (90.21 %). The rate constant of BGT-2 was 15.49 × 10−3 min−1, which was 4.01, 4.16, and 9.22 times higher than those of Bi2MoO6/TiO2 (3.86 × 10−3 min−1), Bi2MoO6 (3.72 × 10−3 min−1), and TiO2 (1.68 ×10−3 min−1), respectively. The degradation efficiencies of amoxicillin and tetracycline hydrochloride were 78.98 % and 92.49 %, respectively. The effects of pH values, water sources, and inorganic anions on CIP degradation were systematically explored. In addition, three environmentally friendly degradation pathways were proposed based on liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) results and the toxicity evaluation of the various intermediates. Testing the optical and electrochemical properties of the catalysts showed that the synthesized dual Z-scheme heterojunctions can significantly improve the light capture ability and reduce the photogenerated electron-hole recombination rate, effectively addressing the challenges encountered with the use of photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The treatment of wastewater discharged from industries associated with medical services, animal husbandry, and aquaculture is of immense importance [1–5]. In particular, ciprofloxacin (CIP), a third-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic, has been widely used in aquaculture [6] and agriculture [7] because of its broad-spectrum activity against bacteria. However, CIP cannot be completely absorbed, and most of it is discharged into aquatic environments, which can spread antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) in bacteria [8]. These consequently pose serious threats to the ecosystem and even human health [9]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop an effective and eco-friendly method to remove CIP from aquatic environments. Unfortunately, conventional methods of CIP removal, such as adsorption [10], biodegradation [11], and Fenton treatment [12], lack sufficient efficiency. Photodegradation is a promising treatment technology for eliminating CIP owing to its highly efficient, economic, and environmentally friendly properties [13].

Since the attempt of Fujishima in 1972 to use titanium dioxide (TiO2) as a photocatalyst to split water under sunlight, TiO2 has received considerable attention because of its non-toxicity, low cost, and chemical stability [14,15]. Various TiO2 nanostructures, such as nanosheets [16], nanoflowers [17], and nanowires [18], have been designed to improve photocatalytic properties. TiO2 nanorod arrays (TiO2 NRs) with one-dimensional structures stand out from numerous nanostructures for their charge collection ability and transfer efficiency [19]. However, the bandgap of TiO2 is so large that most of the solar energy spectrum cannot be efficiently utilized. The most significant limitation affecting its photodegradation efficiency is the high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. These issues have stimulated the development of research strategies to improve the photocatalytic properties of TiO2, with a focus on identifying suitable semiconductors that can be used to build heterojunctions with TiO2. A number of TiO2-based heterojunction photocatalysts have been developed, which have been shown to reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and improve the utilization of sunlight. Yu et al. constructed AgVO3/V2O5-TiO2 heterojunctions to reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and enhance the utilization of visible light in TiO2 NRs [20]. Zhang et al. successfully fabricated heterojunctions of core-shell dual-phase (1 T-2 H)-MoSe2/TiO2 NRs, which effectively reduced the photogenerated charge recombination rate of TiO2 NRs and enhanced the photocatalytic production of hydrogen peroxide [21]. Among semiconductor materials, bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6) is considered suitable for constructing heterojunctions with TiO2. It is an effective and suitable material that can be further investigated using visible light sources, and its structural morphology can be controlled by varying the alternating layers of [Bi2O2]2+ and MoO4 2- [22–24]. Numerous hybrid semiconductors composed of Bi2MoO6 and TiO2 have been studied. These have shown that Bi2MoO6/TiO2 heterojunctions can extend the spectral activity to a wider wavelength range and reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs ( Table 1). However, most studies on Bi2MoO6/TiO2 have focused on traditional type-II heterojunctions, and other types of heterojunctions such as Z-scheme heterojunctions have rarely been studied. Although the charge transfer mode in the type-II heterojunction seems ideal, its ability to successfully inhibit the recombination of photogenerated carriers comes at the cost of weakening the overall redox capacity [25].

To address the challenges encountered with conventional type-II heterojunction photocatalysts, traditional Z-scheme heterojunctions were proposed by Bard in 1979 [31]. The redox couple used in the conventional Z- scheme limits its application to the solution phase. In 2006, Tada et al. first synthesized CdS-Au-TiO2 three-component all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunctions [32]. This scheme utilized a solid conductor instead of shuttling redox ion pairs, making it applicable to both liquids and gases. However, all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalysts are unsuitable for use because of their conductor-dependent charge transfer. Therefore, direct Z-scheme photocatalysts have been developed as a new member of the "Z-scheme" family in which no redox couples and conductors exist. In addition, this photocatalyst design can improve the charge separation efficiency and retain a strong redox capability [33]. Kang's group switched the type-I heterojunction consisting of Bi2S3 and ZnS into a ternary n-ZnS/rGO/p-Bi2S3 all-solid Z-scheme heterojunction using an electron mediator bridge of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [34]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), another graphene derivative, can also be used as carrier transfer intermediates in direct dual Z-scheme heterojunctions, thus reducing the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [35]. GQDs have attracted significant research interest because of their remarkable quantum confinement, edge effects, and extraordinary photoelectric properties [36]. Moreover, they have many potential applications, including in optical sensing, photocatalysis, energy storage, and energy conversion [37].

Herein, ternary Bi2MoO6/GQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts with dual Z-scheme heterojunctions, where GQDs served as the transfer media for photogenerated electrons, were fabricated to improve sunlight utilization and reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The dual Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts exhibited excellent CIP photodegradation performance. The synthesis and photodegradation processes are illustrated in Scheme 1. Photocatalysts grown on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conductive glass are easier to recover than those in powder form and are more suitable for use in large-scale practical production. Various characterization techniques were employed to explore the microstructure, composition, and optical and electrochemical properties of the samples. In addition, the effects of initial pH values, water sources, and inorganic ions were systemically studied. The possible pathways and toxicity of intermediate products in the degradation process were investigated.

All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and were used directly without further refinement. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments. The FTO conductive glass was purchased from Wuhan Jingge Solar Technology Co., Ltd. Tetrabutyl titanate (TBT), ammonium oxalate (AO), the GQD aqueous dispersion solution, and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2) and polyethylene glycol (PEG-600) were obtained from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co. Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3 ·5H2O), sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4 ·2H2O), alcohol (C2H6O), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), CIP, amoxicillin (AMX), and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) were obtained from Macklin Industries Co. Ltd.

The TiO2 NRs were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method based on our previous research [38]. First, an ultrasonically cleaned glass piece (2 cm × 5 cm) was placed in a 50.0 mL Teflon liner with the conductive side facing downwards. Subsequently, 15.0 mL of HCl was slowly added to 15.0 mL of DI water and stirred magnetically for 10 min, followed by the addition of TBT (0.5 mL) with agitation. The solution was then transferred to the Teflon liner containing the conductive glass and kept at 150 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product grown on the FTO glass was washed several times with ethanol and then dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h.

Thereafter, 0.8 mmol Bi (NO3)3 ·5H2O and 0.4 mmol Na2MoO4 ·2H2O were dispersed in C2H6O2 (4.0 mL), and 272.0 mg PEG-600 was dissolved in 8.0 mL C2H6O2. A Bi2MoO6 precursor solution was obtained by mixing the above three solutions with 30.0 mL of ethanol under ultrasonication, followed by the dropwise addition of a variable amount of the GQD solution. The precursor solution was then transferred to the Teflon liner containing the TiO2 NR-loaded FTO glass and reacted at 160 ℃ for 24 h. After cooling, the product was washed several times with ethanol and dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h. The as-prepared ternary catalysts were labeled as BGT-X (X = 1, 2, 3, and 4, representing the addition of 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.4 mL of GQD solution, respectively). For comparison, pristine Bi2MoO6 was synthesized under the same conditions without TiO2 NRs and GQDs. Bi2MoO6/ TiO2 NRs (BT) samples were also prepared using the same process but without GQDs. The masses of the catalysts grown on the glass pieces are listed in Table S1.

All the samples characterized were grown on FTO glass. The crystalline phases and compositions of the as-prepared samples were measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD) under Cu Kα radiation (MAC, M18XHF), and with a laser Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, inVia Reflex). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo, ESCALAB-250) was performed to analyze the elemental states of the materials. The morphologies and microstructures of the as-prepared products were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, S4800) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-F200).

The optical absorption characteristics of the photocatalysts were determined using a UV–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2550). A fluorescence (FL) spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-4500) and an ultrafast time-resolved FL spectrometer (Horiba FluoroMax Plus) were used to determine the photoluminescence (PL) and FL lifetime spectra of the samples as a measure of the charge separation efficiency.

Under the illumination of a Xe lamp (AM 1.5 G, 150 mW/cm2), the transient photocurrent curves were acquired using a three-electrode system with a 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution. The sine wave amplitude was adjusted to 0.01 V, and selected frequencies ranging from 0.1 to 105 Hz were used to obtain the electrochemical impedance spectra. Moreover, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were obtained, and Mott–Schottky curves were evaluated at a frequency of 1000 Hz.

Each sample (2 cm × 5 cm) used in the test was cut into 10 equal parts (1 cm × 1 cm). All the photocatalysts (1 cm × 1 cm) were then immersed in a CIP solution (10 mg/L, 5 mL) in the dark for 2 h to facilitate an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After the start of the photocatalytic reaction, the peak intensity of the CIP solution (at 272 nm) was measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer every 25 min for a total reaction time of 150 min. The solution peak intensity was then converted to the solution concentration using the Beer–Lambert law [39].

In this study, the degradation performance of the samples for CIP was tested under simulated and real sunlight irradiation according to the above experimental procedure. A 300 W Xe lamp was used to simulate sunlight. The CIP degradation performance of the photocatalyst under real sunlight was measured on June 15, 2022. Tap water and river water were used as solvents to prepare the CIP solution (10 mg/L, 5 mL), and the catalytic properties were tested under simulated sunlight according to the experimental procedure described above. Tap water was obtained from the Hefei City Water Treatment Plant, while river water was collected from the Shiwuli River in Hefei City, both of which were used directly without re-treatment.

Trapping experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR; Bruker, EMXplus-6/1) signals were used to determine the active species in the photodegradation process. Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS, Thermo Fisher, LTQ Orbitrap XL) was used to explore the intermediates in CIP photodegradation. The LC-MS test parameters are provided in the Supporting information. The stability of the catalysts was estimated by cycling experiments and comparing their XRD patterns before and after degradation.

The XRD patterns of all as-prepared samples are presented in Fig. 1(a) and Fig. S1, and were used to analyze the components and crystal phases. For TiO2 NRs, diffraction peaks are located at 35.70°, 54.00°, 62.42°, and 69.54°, which are indexed to the (101), (211), (002), and (112) crystal planes of the TiO2 rutile phase (JCPDS No. 73–1765), respectively. The peaks at 28.08°, 32.53°, and 46.73° correspond to the (131), (200), and (202) crystal planes of Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS No. 72–1524), respectively. Characteristic peaks of both TiO2 NRs and Bi2MoO6 are observed in the XRD patterns of BT and BGT. However, no diffraction peaks of GQDs are detected in the XRD patterns of BGT, owing to their low concentration [40].

Fig. 1(b) shows the Raman spectra of the samples. The relevant Raman peak data are presented in Table S2. In the Raman spectrum of the TiO2 NRs, three peaks (denoted as T1, T2, and T3) in the range of 200–650 cm−1 can be seen; these are assigned to the multi-phonon processes, Eg, and A1 g [41]. In the BT spectrum, characteristic peaks of Bi2MoO6 are observed at 688 cm−1, 797 cm−1, and 891 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching of (Bi2O2)2+, symmetric stretching of MoO4 2− (Oap), and symmetric ν 1(A1) of MoO4 2− [42]. In the case of BGT-2, apart from the characteristic peaks of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6, characteristic peaks of GQDs are seen at 1098 cm−1, 1210 cm−1, 1346 cm−1, 1457 cm−1, 1575 cm−1, and 1807 cm−1, which are labeled as D1, D2, D, D3, G, and D4 [43], respectively. In particular, the D and G peaks arise from defects at the edges of the graphene and graphite platelets and the E2 g phonon of sp2 bonds between the carbon atoms. Therefore, the above results demonstrate the successful addition of GQDs to BGT-2.

The surface morphology of pure TiO2 is shown in Fig. 1(c), which indicates that the TiO2 NRs vertically grow on the FTO substrate. Fig. 1(d) shows extremely thin Bi2MoO6 nanosheets with an average thickness of 15 nm. The SEM image of BT (Fig. 1(e)) shows Bi2MoO6 nanoflowers assembled from nanosheets on top of the TiO2 NRs. By comparison, the micrograph for BGT-2 is the same as that for BT without GQD doping, which indicates that the GQDs are physically loaded on TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 (Fig. 1(f–g)). However, the GQDs could not be imaged because of the limitations of the SEM equipment [44].

TEM and HRTEM images were used to further characterize the microstructure of BGT-2. From Fig. 1(h), it is evident that the Bi2MoO6 nanosheets and GQDs are attached to the TiO2 NRs. The HRTEM images of BGT-2 are shown in Fig. 1(i–k). The distribution of the GQDs is shown in Fig. 1(i). Interplanar spacings of 0.324 and 0.249 nm are observed, corresponding to the d-spacings of the (110) and (101) planes in the TiO2 rutile phase. In addition, some Bi2MoO6 nanosheets with a distinct lattice fringe of 0.315 nm are observed. Some globular nanostructures 5.000 nm in diameter are interpreted as typical GQDs with an interplanar spacing of 0.210 nm [45]. Hence, the above results confirm the GQD-induced modification of the nanostructure of ternary heterojunctions. The TEM-EDS elemental (C, O, Ti, Mo, and Bi) mappings of BGT-2 are shown in Fig. S2. Fig. S3 shows the EDS spectrum of BGT-2.

XPS patterns were used to analyze the components and oxidation states of TiO2, Bi2MoO6, and BGT-2. The survey spectra are displayed in Fig. 2 (a), demonstrating the presence of Ti, Bi, Mo, O, and C in BGT-2 compared with the full spectra of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the peaks of Ti 2p in the TiO2 spectrum centered at 458.55 eV and 464.30 eV corresponding to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 states of Ti4+ in pure TiO2 [46]. Moreover, two Ti 2p peaks in BGT-2 shift to lower energy positions at 458.21 eV and 463.96 eV. In Fig. 2(c), the two peaks at 159.25 eV and 164.50 eV can be attributed to multiple Bi 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 binding energies, matching with the Bi3+ of Bi2MoO6 [47]. Additionally, the Bi peaks of BGT-2 exhibit lower binding energy than those of Bi2MoO6. In Fig. 2(d), the Mo 3d peaks of Bi2MoO6 at 232.15 eV and 235.35 eV correspond to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, indicating the existence of Mo6+ in Bi2MoO6. The Mo 3d peaks of BGT-2 at 232.00 eV and 235.10 eV are in lower binding energy positions than those of Bi2MoO6. These shifts in Ti 2p, Bi 4f, and Mo 3d suggest an increased electron cloudy density [48]. Subsequently, the relevant O 1s and C 1 s data were further analyzed and are displayed in Fig. 2(e) and (f). In the O 1s spectra, the structures of the Bi-O, Mo-O, and Ti-O bands are fitted at 529.90 eV, 530.60 eV, and 531.60 eV, respectively. The C1s peak is divided into three peaks at 284.80 eV, 286.10 eV, and 288.40 eV, which are respectively assigned to C-C/CC/C-H, C-O/C-OH, and O-CO [49].

The photocatalytic performance of CIP is shown in Fig. 3. In Fig. 3(a), the ternary heterojunctions exhibit enhanced photodegradation performance compared to TiO2, Bi2MoO6, and BT. BGT-2 is identified as the optimal photocatalyst with the highest CIP photodegradation efficiency of 90.21 %. In Fig. 3(b), the curves of ln(C0/C)–t are used to estimate the photodegradation kinetics, which perfectly corresponds to the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation. Fig. 3(c) shows the photodegradation rates and kinetic constants of all materials. The rate constant of BGT-2 is 15.49 × 10−3 min−1, which is 4.01, 4.16, and 9.22 times higher than those of Bi2MoO6/TiO2 (3.86 ×10−3 min−1), Bi2MoO6 (3.72 ×10−3 min−1), and TiO2 (1.68 ×10−3 min−1), respectively. The photocatalytic activity of BGT-2 is confirmed by the gradual decrease in the maximum absorption intensity of the CIP solution until the peak disappears (Fig. S4).

To investigate the degradation performance of the catalysts for CIP under solar light, we obtained data for the degradation of the CIP solution by TiO2, Bi2MoO6, BT, and BGT-2. The entire dark adsorption and testing process is the same as that performed using the Xe lamp; only the light source differs (i.e., changed to sunlight). As shown in Fig. S5(a), BGT-2 exhibits superior performance for CIP degradation under sunlight, indicating that the ternary heterojunction constructed in this study can improve utilization of sunlight compared to monolithic and binary catalysts. Fig. S5 (b) shows the kinetic curves of the four catalysts for CIP degradation. The kinetics of photocatalysis can be represented by a first-order kinetic equation. As shown in Fig. 5S(c), the degradation efficiencies of TiO2, Bi2MoO6, BT, and BGT-2 are 48.27 %, 50.22 %, 70.69 %, and 89.03 %, respectively. Additionally, their rate constants are 4.39 × 10−3 min−1, 4.65 × 10−3 min−1, 8.18 × 10−3 min−1, and 14.73 × 10−3 min−1, respectively.

The photodegradation efficiency of the CIP solution under various pH conditions and inorganic ions was estimated and is shown in Fig. 4. The initial pH values played a significant role in the structure of CIP owing to protonation and deprotonation. The pKa1 and pKa2 of CIP are 6.1 and 8.6, respectively. Therefore, when the pH is less than 6.1, CIP protonates as a cation; when the pH is in the range of 6.1–8.6, CIP exists in a neutral state; and when the pH is greater than 8.62, CIP exists mainly as an anion [50]. From Fig. S6, the zero-point charge (pHpzc) of BGT-2 is 7.2. In other words, when the pH is 7.2, there was no charge on the catalyst surface. When the pH is less than 7.2, the catalyst surface is positively charged; otherwise, it is negatively charged. Therefore, the adsorption rate of CIP on the photocatalyst surface is reduced by the repulsive force between homogeneous charges in acidic and alkaline solutions, thus affecting the CIP degradation efficiency of the photocatalyst. As seen in Fig. 4(a), the degradation efficiency of CIP is lowered under acidic (pH ≤ 5.0) and alkaline (pH ≥ 9.0) conditions, which is consistent with the above findings. In addition, the natural pH of the CIP solution is 6.1. Fig. 4 shows that the CIP degradation efficiency of BGT-2 under neutral conditions is higher than that at pH 6.1. Therefore, a pH of 7.0 was maintained throughout this experiment. Fig. 4(c) shows that the CIP photodegradation efficiency varies under the impact of various inorganic ions. The degradation efficiency slightly decreases after the addition of Cl- and NO3 - to the CIP solution. It is evident that the addition of CO3 2- and HPO4 2- restricts the photocatalytic performance, which is mainly attributed to the conversion of inorganic ions to less-reactive radicals produced by the photogenerated holes and radical species [51]. Kinetic curves are plotted in Fig. 4(b) and (d), and correspond to a pseudo-first-order kinetic equation.

For practical applications, it is essential to investigate the photocatalytic capabilities of CIP in various aquatic environments. Accordingly, the CIP photodegradation efficiency using tap water and river water was estimated, and the results are shown in Fig. 5(a). Real-world water sources reduce the degradation efficiency of CIP owing to the complexity of the water quality. From Fig. 5(b), the rate constants of CIP in DI, tap, and river water are 15.49 × 10−3 min−1, 12.60 × 10−3 min−1, and 6.49 × 10−3 min−1, respectively. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 5(c), the degradation rates of AMX, CIP, and TC-HCl (10 mg/L of each antibiotic) are 78.98 %, 90.21 %, and 92.49 %, respectively, suggesting the applicability of the as-prepared photocatalysts for antibiotics. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the photodegradation kinetics of each antibiotic fits the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation.

The stability of BGT-2 was estimated using four cycling tests, and the results are displayed in Fig. 6(a). After four cycles, the CIP removal rates vary only slightly. In addition, the XRD patterns in Fig. 6(b) for BGT-2 remain mostly unchanged after photodegradation.

UV–vis absorption spectra were used to evaluate the light-harvesting properties of the as-synthesized photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the absorption intensities of the ternary samples are generally higher than those of the other materials. Moreover, the absorption edge of BGT-2 is redshifted compared with those of TiO2 and BT. These two changes evince the high conversion efficiency of solar energy to chemical energy. Based on the UV–vis absorption spectra, the bandgaps of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 can be calculated using Tauc’s equation as follows [52]:
(1) (αhν)2 = C (hν - Eg)
where α, hν, C, and Eg are the absorption coefficient, photon energy, a constant, and the bandgap, respectively. The bandgaps of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 (Fig. 7(b)) are 3.00 eV and 2.70 eV, respectively. To eliminate the difference in the contact potential between the test samples and XPS analyzer, the valence band (VB) positions of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 can be obtained based on the following formula [53]:
(2) EVB = EVB-XPS + φ - 4·44
where EVB, EVB-XPS, and φ are the VB potential, VB potential measured by the VB XPS spectra, and the work function of the XPS analyzer (4.55 eV), respectively. From Fig. 7(c), the EVB-XPS values of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 are 2.67 eV and 2.01 eV. Hence, the EVB values of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 are 2.78 eV and 2.12 eV, respectively. Fig. 7(d) shows the band structures of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 based on the above results.

The separation efficiency and lifetime of the photogenerated charges were evaluated using PL and FL lifetime spectra [54]. In Fig. 8, the BGT-2 heterojunctions exhibit the lowest FL intensities and longest FL lifetime among the detected samples, indicating the high separation efficiency and long lifespan of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

The electrochemical performance of all samples was measured using a three-electrode system with a 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution. The transient photocurrent responses were investigated with an intermittent on-off light switch, and the relevant data are shown in Fig. 9(a). BGT-2 exhibits the highest current density among the tested samples, demonstrating its outstanding separation efficiency for photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Fig. 9(b) shows the impedance curve for each sample fitted according to the equivalent circuit in the upper-left corner of the figure. The smaller the radius of the impedance curve, the higher the conductivity of the sample [55]. Compared with TiO2, Bi2MoO6, and BT, the radius of BGT-2 is the smallest, indicative of the minimum transfer resistance; this corresponds with the PL spectral results. The LSV curves are shown in Fig. 9(c). BGT-2 requires the least potential to reach a given current density compared to the other catalysts. The current density of BGT-2 is significantly enhanced compared to the other samples, indicating that the increased interface of the ternary heterojunction increases the carrier migration rate. An increase in the carrier mobility is beneficial as more reactive sites are available, thus improving the performance of the catalyst [56]. Furthermore, the flat band potentials of pure TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 were detected using the Mott–Schottky curves in Fig. 9(d). The positive slopes of pure TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 indicate that both are n-type semiconductors. The flat band potentials of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 are −0.12 V and −0.46 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively.

The possible degradation pathways during CIP degradation are shown in Fig. 10. The relevant data for the intermediates are shown in Fig. S7 and Table S3. In pathway 1, CIP is attacked by superoxide or hydroxide free radicals due to dealkylation and deamination reactions [57]. The F atom is then cleared to form P2 (m/z = 245). P2 is converted to P3 (m/z = 123) by cleavage of the amino group, cyclopropyl group, and carboxylation. The piperazine ring of CIP is broken by superoxide free radicals and follows two possible degradation pathways [58]. In pathway 2, P6 (m/z = 315) is formed from P4 (m/z = 362) by the removal of F atoms and carbonyl groups. P6 is converted to P2 via de-carbonylation and N-dealkylation. P2 is then cleared via the route P2→P3→small atoms. In the other pathway, P4 loses its amino group and undergoes carbonylation to produce P8 (m/z = 290). Consequently, P8 becomes P9 (m/z = 272) due to the loss of the F atom. The degradation products are CO2, H2O, and other small molecules.

As research on photocatalysis has advanced, it has become essential to assess the toxicity of the intermediate products of pollutant degradation processes [59,60]. In this study, the toxicity of CIP and intermediates in the degradation process was explored using the Toxicity Estimation Software TOOL (T.E.S.T.) [61]. Two indices, the mutagenicity and bioconcentration factor, were predicted according to the consensus method. As shown in Fig. 11(a), compared with CIP, the mutagenicity of the intermediates is significantly reduced, except for P9. Most of the intermediates (P1, P2, P3, P6, and P8) are “mutagenicity negative” [62]. As shown in Fig. 11(b), the bioconcentration factors of all intermediates are inferior to that of CIP, indicating that the breaking of the piperazine ring would involve a loss of toxicity. Therefore, the degradation of CIP by the BGT heterojunction results in toxicity attenuation, with potential for application in treating CIP-containing wastewater.

EPR and trapping experiments were performed to study the active species in CIP degradation. DMPO was chosen as the spin-trapping reagent for ·OH and ·O2 - [63]. Fig. 12(a-b) shows that there are no significant characteristic peaks of DMPO- ·OH and DMPO- ·O2 - adducts without light irradiation. However, DMPO- ·OH and DMPO- ·O2 - adduct peaks are observed under illumination. In addition, with increasing light duration, the intensity of the peaks is maintained at high levels. As displayed in Fig. 12(c-d), three scavengers (IPA, AO, and BQ) are used to capture the active species of ·OH, h+, and ·O2 -, respectively. After the addition of AO and BQ to the CIP solution, the photodegradation rates are 68.35 % and 34.93 % lower than those of the CIP solution with IPA (89.23 %) and that without scavengers (90.21 %). Therefore, ·OH, h+, and ·O2 - play important roles in CIP degradation. In particular, ·O2 - is the primary active species in the process.

Based on the band structure of the as-prepared photocatalysts, a traditional type-II mechanism was first introduced to analyze the photocatalytic reactions. As shown in Fig. 13(a), the h+ in the VBs of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 is transferred to the VB of the GQDs. Additionally, the oxidation potentials of OH−/ ·OH (1.99 V vs. NHE) and H2O/·OH (2.34 V vs. NHE) are higher than the VB potential of GQDs at 1.00 V. As a result, ·OH cannot be generated by h+ in the VB of GQDs, which contradicts the results of the trapping experiments and EPR spectra [64]. Therefore, Fig. 13(b) presents a dual Z-scheme carrier transfer mode, in which GQDs are recognized as transporting media to both, produce and accept electrons. The photogenerated electrons in the conduction bands (CBs) of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 directly recombine with the holes in the VB of the GQDs, which can result in electrons being stored in the CB of the GQDs and holes in the VB of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6. ·O2 - can be generated by the electrons in the CB of the GQDs, while the holes retained in the VB of TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 can yield ·OH. Herein, the dual Z-scheme charge transfer mode can be used to explain the enhanced photocatalytic performance via the reduced recombination of electron-hole pairs and increased carrier transfer efficiency. The corresponding CIP photodegradation mechanism is described by Eqs. (3–9).
(3) TiO2 + hν → TiO2 (e-) + TiO2 (h+)
(4) Bi2MoO6 + hν → Bi2MoO6 (e-) + Bi2MoO6 (h+)
(5) GQDs + hν → GQDs (e-) + GQDs (h+)
(6) TiO2 (e-) + Bi2MoO6 (e-) + GQDs (h+) → TiO2 + Bi2MoO6 + GQDs
(7) GQDs (e-) + O2 → ·O2 -
(8) TiO2 (h+) + H2O/OH- → ·OH
(9) Bi2MoO6 (h+) +OH- → ·OH

A comparison of the CIP degradation properties of different photocatalysts is presented in Table 2. The superiority of BGT for the photodegradation of CIP is demonstrated by the high catalytic efficiency obtained in a short time with low catalyst usage. Moreover, the heterojunction system can be easily recovered.

In this work, a recyclable Bi2MoO6/GQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst with a dual Z-scheme heterojunction was successfully synthesized and exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance in a CIP solution. BGT-2 exhibited the highest CIP photodegradation efficiency (90.21 %). The rate constant of BGT-2 was 15.49 × 10−3 min−1, which was 4.01, 4.16, and 9.22 times higher than those of Bi2MoO6/TiO2 (3.86 × 10−3 min−1), Bi2MoO6 (3.72 × 10−3 min−1), and TiO2 (1.68 × 10−3 min−1), respectively. The AMX and TC-HCl degradation rates were 78.98 % and 92.49 %, respectively. The effects of pH values, water sources, and inorganic anions on CIP degradation were systemically explored, providing a credible basis for selecting optimal degradation conditions. Three possible pathways for CIP degradation were proposed based on the LC-MS results. Trapping experiments and EPR spectra showed that ·OH, h+, and ·O2 - played significant roles in CIP degradation. A dual Z-scheme carrier transfer mode was introduced, in which the GQDs were regarded as electron-transporting media. The excellent photodegradation properties of the heterojunction could be attributed to two factors: (1) the extensive absorption range under sunlight, which enhanced the conversion efficiency of solar energy, and (2) the limited recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Based on the outstanding degradation performance, stability, and convenient recovery, we confirmed that the ternary Bi2MoO6/GQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst has promising industrial applications in the photodegradation of antibiotics.
